quote: I'm okay. I'm like, who's calling me from South Carolina?
stewart_read: Deb's opening question is a mild legitimacy challenge — she doesn't know who Ion is or why a South Carolina number is calling her. Meg responds with 'Yep. That's me. So sorry about that.' — an apology rather than a legitimacy anchor. The codex's intros.standard coaching note calls for referencing the form submission to legitimize the call; Meg does mention the form in her intro but then apologizes for the caller ID rather than using it as a trust moment ('You filled out a form about solar — that's how I have your info, and that's my work line calling from our office'). The apology is not damaging, but it's a missed micro-trade on a customer who is already mildly suspicious.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg's apology was a deliberate softener to reduce friction at the top of the call — a reasonable choice with a neutral-toned customer.
coaching_implication: Show Meg the codex's protocols.how_did_you_get_my_number template. The move is specificity, not apology — 'You filled out a form online about solar, that's how I have your info' answers the implicit question and builds legitimacy in one line. The apology concedes ground that didn't need to be conceded.

quote: Well, I'm not really ready to do this. That's why I just wanted the estimate.
stewart_read: This is the call's pivotal moment. Deb's 'not ready' framing is a pre-decisional deflection, not a hard objection — she filled out the form, she's buying a house, she wants to understand solar. The codex's objection_inversion principle applies: the flip available here is that the design conversation IS the estimate she wants. Meg instead responds 'For sure. Okay.' and pivots to logistics. Strategic read: Meg may have judged that pushing on a pre-close customer would create friction. Reactive read: Meg accepted the customer's frame without testing whether curiosity could be created. The reactive read is more supported — Meg never attempts any reframe across the full call, not just here.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg read Deb as genuinely pre-qualified (pre-close, inspection pending) and chose not to push, preserving the relationship for the May callback.
coaching_implication: Walk Meg through the objection_inversion move for 'not ready' situations: the design conversation is exactly what gives Deb the information she needs to plan. Suggested reframe intent (not verbatim): 'That's actually perfect timing — the design we build now gives you a baseline so you know exactly what you're working with once the inspection comes back.' No fabricated numbers needed; the value is the planning tool itself.

quote: So is it better to go ahead and have a roof replaced, or is it better to wait and do that at the same time you're gonna install panels?
stewart_read: Deb is asking an unsolicited, specific question about roof-and-solar sequencing — this is a genuine engagement signal from a customer who just said she wasn't ready. She is thinking through the project, not deflecting. This is the highest-value moment in the call commercially: Deb has a house from the 1940s, an almost-certain roof replacement need, and is actively asking how to coordinate it with solar. The codex's cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned template applies directly.
coaching_implication: Meg should have treated this question as the opening to route to the roofing team in parallel — not just mention the bundle and defer to the callback. The codex's cross_sell template calls for: 'Ion does roofing in-house, both standalone AND as the first phase of a solar install — want me to have our roofing team take a look?' That routing move captures a potential roofing sale regardless of whether the solar appointment ever happens. Show Meg the SESSION18_fcbf33c1 positive example (Kelly bridged in 4 seconds).

quote: Yeah. We also are able to do roof replacements here at Ion. We do, like, a bundle option. So if that's something we need to do, you know, we could always explore that when I call you back in a couple weeks.
stewart_read: Meg is aware of the bundle and names it — this is not a rep who missed the product entirely. The gap is in execution: she defers the roofing conversation to the May callback rather than routing to the roofing team in parallel now. A 1940s house with an uninspected roof and a customer actively asking about sequencing is a roofing lead today, independent of whether solar closes in May. The alternative hypothesis — that routing is premature given Deb hasn't closed on the house yet — has some merit, but the codex's cross_sell_signals section explicitly calls for co-routing to the roofing team in real time. A roofing consultation doesn't require Deb to own the house yet; it requires her interest, which she has demonstrated.
alternative_hypothesis: Routing to the roofing team before Deb has closed on the house and completed inspection is premature — the roofing team would have nothing actionable to work with yet.
coaching_implication: The distinction to make with Meg: mentioning the bundle is not the same as routing the lead. The roofing team can have a preliminary conversation with Deb about what to expect, what questions to ask the inspector, and how the bundle works — that conversation has value before close. Coach Meg on the difference between 'I mentioned it' and 'I handed it off.' The codex's revenue_impact note on this signal is 'high — captures both immediate roofing revenue AND future solar revenue.'

quote: It it all depends on what I'm gonna have to replace and all of that. So that's why I just kinda wanted to get an idea.
stewart_read: This is the second instance of Deb's pre-decisional deflection (see multi_touch_patterns). Meg again accommodates rather than reframes. The codex's curiosity_over_features principle is directly applicable: Deb is describing exactly the situation where a design conversation has value — she doesn't know what she'll need, and a design gives her a planning baseline. Meg's response is to offer a soft callback, which accepts Deb's framing that the design conversation should wait until after the inspection.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg's soft callback is a reasonable holding pattern for a genuinely pre-close customer — pushing for an appointment before Deb owns the house could damage the relationship.
coaching_implication: The coaching point is not 'push harder' — it's 'reframe the value of the conversation.' A design consultation as a planning tool (not a sales pitch) is something Deb can use right now to prepare for the inspection. Meg never offers that frame. Walk her through how curiosity_over_features applies to pre-decisional customers specifically.

quote: And I I use almost no gas except for a backup backup gas heater, in a couple of months out of the year.
stewart_read: Deb is describing a heat-pump-primary home with minimal gas use — this is a high-electricity-load profile that makes solar economics favorable. She is volunteering this information unprompted, which signals she has already been thinking about the fit. Meg acknowledges with 'Mhmm' and does not anchor on it. The codex's bill_anchoring principle calls for capturing AND flipping the bill into emotional alignment — Meg never asks for the current bill amount, never anchors on the heat pump load, and never creates curiosity about what the design would show for this specific usage profile.
coaching_implication: Deb handed Meg a bill-anchor opportunity: a heat-pump home with low gas use is exactly the customer profile where solar offsets the largest share of total energy cost. Meg should have asked for the current electric bill and used it as the curiosity hook — 'With a heat pump running most of your load, your electric bill is probably doing most of the work. What does that look like right now?' No fabricated numbers; the hook is the question itself.

quote: Perfect. What I can do is, I can just check that maybe towards, like, the May.
stewart_read: This is not a booked appointment — it is a soft callback promise with no locked date, no time, no confirmation of what Deb should expect, and no 'is that fair' handshake. The codex's button_up section requires appointment datetime confirmed, one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving-at-computer anchor, and the verbal handshake. None of these elements are present. The outcome is a vague intention to call back 'maybe towards, like, the May' — the hedging language ('maybe,' 'like') signals even Meg is not treating this as a firm commitment.
alternative_hypothesis: Given Deb's pre-close status, a soft callback is the only realistic outcome — a formal appointment structure would be premature.
coaching_implication: Even a callback should have a locked structure. Coach Meg on the difference between 'I'll call you in May' and 'Let me put you on the calendar for the first week of May — what day works for you?' A locked callback date dramatically improves contact rate. The button_up protocol applies to callbacks as much as appointments.
